unitDescription.value: In this unit, students explore sustainable living through the lens of resilience and self-sufficiency, examining how designed solutions can support survival and wellbeing in extreme or off-grid scenarios. Students investigate sustainability from ecological, economic, and social perspectives, considering how ethical decision-making, resource management, and long-term impacts influence design choices. Through analysis of real-world sustainable systems such as energy, water, food production, and shelter, students develop an understanding of how designers and engineers respond to challenges related to security, sustainability, and human needs. The unit emphasises thoughtful planning, innovation, and the responsible use of technologies to create sustainable living environments.

description.value: In this assessment, students design a sustainable doomsday shelter in response to a given scenario that requires living off the grid for an extended period of time. Students analyse the needs and constraints of the scenario, develop a design brief, and investigate systems and resources related to shelter, energy, water, food, and waste management. Using SketchUp, students create and refine a digital model of their shelter, justifying design decisions with reference to ecological, economic, and social sustainability considerations. Students evaluate their design against developed criteria, including sustainability and practicality, and communicate their solution using digital tools while demonstrating effective project planning and safe, appropriate use of technologies.

conditions.value: 2–3 A3 pages or equivalent digital media pages that may include annotated graphical representations

achievementStandard.value: By the end of Year 10 students explain how people consider factors that impact on design decisions and the technologies used to design and produce products, services and environments for sustainable living. They explain the contribution of innovation, enterprise skills and emerging technologies to global preferred futures. For one or more of the technologies contexts, students explain the features of technologies and their appropriateness for purpose, and create designed solutions based on an analysis of needs or opportunities. Students create, adapt and refine design ideas, processes and solutions and justify their decisions against developed design criteria that include sustainability. They communicate design ideas, processes and solutions to a range of audiences, including using digital tools. Students independently and collaboratively develop and apply production and project management plans, adjusting processes when necessary. They select and use technologies skilfully and safely to produce designed solutions.

description.value: In this assessment, students complete an examination that assesses their understanding of key sustainability principles. The exam includes questions on how designers analyse needs and opportunities, consider ethical and sustainability factors. Students demonstrate knowledge of ecological, economical and societal sustainability as well as sketching and representation techniques. This assessment focuses on students’ ability to explain and apply foundational sustainability design knowledge across product, service and/or environmental design contexts.

conditions.value:
• up to 50 minutes plus 10 minutes planning time
• under supervised conditions
• short response up to 100 words per item, up to 600 words for the task
• extended response 200–300 words per item, up to 600 words for the task

achievementStandard.value: By the end of Year 10 students explain how people consider factors that impact on design decisions and the technologies used to design and produce products, services and environments for sustainable living. They explain the contribution of innovation, enterprise skills and emerging technologies to global preferred futures. For one or more of the technologies contexts, students explain the features of technologies and their appropriateness for purpose, and create designed solutions based on an analysis of needs or opportunities. Students create, adapt and refine design ideas, processes and solutions and justify their decisions against developed design criteria that include sustainability. They communicate design ideas, processes and solutions to a range of audiences, including using digital tools. subElements.value:
Inquiring: Identify, process and evaluate information
Generating: Create possibilities; Put ideas into action
Analysing: Interpret concepts and problems; Draw conclusions and provide reasons; Evaluate actions and outcomes
Reflecting: Transfer knowledge

Investigating: Locate information; Interpret data
Creating and exchanging: Plan; Create, communicate and collaborate
Managing and operating: Select and operate tools

keyTeachingExperiences.value:
Theory: Introduction to Unit 2: Sustainability and the context of resilient, off-grid living. Students explore the three pillars of sustainability—ecological, economic, and social—and how these apply to extreme scenarios. Analysis of the 'Doomsday Shelter' project scenario, focusing on identifying human needs (water, food, energy, shelter) and the constraints of a self-sufficient environment. 

Prac: Collaborative brainstorming and decomposition of the design problem. Students create mind maps to identify the interdependencies between survival systems and ethical considerations in resource management.

resources.value: Unit outline, project scenario handout, sustainability framework infographics, video case studies on off-grid resilience.

theory.value: Introduction to Unit 2: Sustainability and the context of resilient, off-grid living. Students explore the three pillars of sustainability—ecological, economic, and social—and how these apply to extreme scenarios. Analysis of the 'Doomsday Shelter' project scenario, focusing on identifying human needs (water, food, energy, shelter) and the constraints of a self-sufficient environment.

prac.value: Collaborative brainstorming and decomposition of the design problem. Students create mind maps to identify the interdependencies between survival systems and ethical considerations in resource management.

assessment.value: Formative: Initial identification of needs and constraints for the Doomsday Shelter project.

outlineTheme.value: Introduction to sustainability frameworks and off-grid living principles.

keyTeachingExperiences.value:
Theory: Investigation of sustainable systems including passive solar design, rainwater harvesting, and renewable energy generation. Students examine how environmental factors such as climate, topography, and site orientation influence design decisions. Introduction to life cycle thinking and the environmental impact of material selection in construction.

Prac: Site analysis and conceptual sketching. Students research and evaluate existing sustainable technologies to determine suitability for their shelter design. Development of system diagrams illustrating the flow of resources (water, energy, waste) within the proposed solution.

resources.value: Climate data tools, technical articles on sustainable building materials, technical drawing equipment, design journals.

theory.value: Investigation of sustainable systems including passive solar design, rainwater harvesting, and renewable energy generation. Students examine how environmental factors such as climate, topography, and site orientation influence design decisions. Introduction to life cycle thinking and the environmental impact of material selection in construction.

prac.value: Site analysis and conceptual sketching. Students research and evaluate existing sustainable technologies to determine suitability for their shelter design. Development of system diagrams illustrating the flow of resources (water, energy, waste) within the proposed solution.

assessment.value: Formative: Peer review of conceptual system diagrams and research findings.

outlineTheme.value: Investigating human needs and resource management in extreme environments.

keyTeachingExperiences.value:
Theory: Introduction to 3D digital representation techniques for environmental design. Students learn the importance of scale and perspective in communicating complex engineered solutions. Explicit teaching on developing a design brief, including the establishment of success criteria for evaluation.

Prac: Introductory SketchUp workshop focusing on 3D workspace navigation, basic geometry creation, and scaling. Students begin drafting their formal design brief, articulating how their proposed shelter will address the identified ecological and social needs of the scenario.

resources.value: SketchUp software, 3D modelling tutorial guides, design brief templates, digital representation examples.

theory.value: Introduction to 3D digital representation techniques for environmental design. Students learn the importance of scale and perspective in communicating complex engineered solutions. Explicit teaching on developing a design brief, including the establishment of success criteria for evaluation.

prac.value: Introductory SketchUp workshop focusing on 3D workspace navigation, basic geometry creation, and scaling. Students begin drafting their formal design brief, articulating how their proposed shelter will address the identified ecological and social needs of the scenario.

assessment.value: Formative: Draft design brief submission for teacher feedback.

outlineTheme.value: Analysis of renewable energy and water harvesting technologies for self-sufficiency.

keyTeachingExperiences.value:
Theory: Investigation of off-grid energy and water systems. Students explore renewable energy technologies including photovoltaic systems, wind turbines, and battery storage, focusing on load calculations for survival scenarios. Principles of potable water management are examined, including atmospheric water generation, harvesting, and multi-stage filtration (UV, mechanical, and biological).

Prac: Students conduct a resource audit based on their chosen 'Doomsday' scenario, calculating daily kilowatt-hour requirements and water consumption. They use local Yeppoon meteorological data to determine the viability of solar and rainwater harvesting systems for their specific shelter site.

resources.value: Climate data sets, renewable energy specification sheets, water filtration diagrams, solar yield calculators, energy audit templates.

theory.value: Investigation of off-grid energy and water systems. Students explore renewable energy technologies including photovoltaic systems, wind turbines, and battery storage, focusing on load calculations for survival scenarios. Principles of potable water management are examined, including atmospheric water generation, harvesting, and multi-stage filtration (UV, mechanical, and biological).

prac.value: Students conduct a resource audit based on their chosen 'Doomsday' scenario, calculating daily kilowatt-hour requirements and water consumption. They use local Yeppoon meteorological data to determine the viability of solar and rainwater harvesting systems for their specific shelter site.

assessment.value: Formative: Research portfolio entry documenting selected energy and water technologies with justification based on ecological and practical sustainability.

outlineTheme.value: Developing the design brief and analysing shelter scenario constraints.

keyTeachingExperiences.value:
Theory: Examination of closed-loop food and waste systems. Students investigate intensive food production methods such as aquaponics and vertical gardening, considering the nutritional requirements for long-term survival. Waste management strategies are explored through the lens of the circular economy, focusing on biogas digesters, composting toilets, and nutrient recycling to maintain soil health.

Prac: Students develop a systemic flowchart representing the 'inputs and outputs' of their shelter. They design a nutrient cycle that links human waste management back to food production, identifying how technologies can minimise environmental impact while ensuring social sustainability.

resources.value: Permaculture design principles, aquaponics system models, biogas technical guides, nutritional requirement charts.

theory.value: Examination of closed-loop food and waste systems. Students investigate intensive food production methods such as aquaponics and vertical gardening, considering the nutritional requirements for long-term survival. Waste management strategies are explored through the lens of the circular economy, focusing on biogas digesters, composting toilets, and nutrient recycling to maintain soil health.

prac.value: Students develop a systemic flowchart representing the 'inputs and outputs' of their shelter. They design a nutrient cycle that links human waste management back to food production, identifying how technologies can minimise environmental impact while ensuring social sustainability.

assessment.value: Formative: Peer review of system flowcharts, focusing on the logic of resource recovery and waste reduction.

outlineTheme.value: Generating and representing design ideas using divergent graphical techniques.

keyTeachingExperiences.value:
Theory: Introduction to digital representation and spatial zoning. Students learn about the functional zoning of living environments (clean vs. dirty zones, private vs. communal) and the importance of scale and orientation in passive solar design. Introduction to 3D modelling conventions in SketchUp, including groups, components, and the use of layers to manage complex engineered systems.

Prac: Initial digital prototyping in SketchUp. Students translate their conceptual sketches into a scaled 3D footprint, focusing on wall thickness, thermal mass, and internal layout. They begin applying 'tags' to distinguish between structural elements and service systems (energy/water).

resources.value: SketchUp Pro, 3D Warehouse for components, architectural scale guides, passive design orientation diagrams, student laptops.

theory.value: Introduction to digital representation and spatial zoning. Students learn about the functional zoning of living environments (clean vs. dirty zones, private vs. communal) and the importance of scale and orientation in passive solar design. Introduction to 3D modelling conventions in SketchUp, including groups, components, and the use of layers to manage complex engineered systems.

prac.value: Initial digital prototyping in SketchUp. Students translate their conceptual sketches into a scaled 3D footprint, focusing on wall thickness, thermal mass, and internal layout. They begin applying 'tags' to distinguish between structural elements and service systems (energy/water).

assessment.value: Formative: Progress check of SketchUp model focusing on accuracy of scale and logical spatial organisation.

outlineTheme.value: Digital 3D modelling and representation of environmental design solutions.

keyTeachingExperiences.value:
Theory: Investigation of off-grid technological systems required for long-term survival, focusing on the integration of renewable energy (photovoltaics and wind), atmospheric water generation, and closed-loop waste management. Students examine the engineering principles of structural integrity for resilient environments.

Prac: Application of digital modelling skills using SketchUp. Students begin translating 2D conceptual sketches into a 3D environment, focusing on accurate scaling and the placement of critical life-support systems. Instruction on the use of components and layers to organise complex structural data.

resources.value: SketchUp Pro, CAD workstations, off-grid system technical specifications, structural design tutorials.

theory.value: Investigation of off-grid technological systems required for long-term survival, focusing on the integration of renewable energy (photovoltaics and wind), atmospheric water generation, and closed-loop waste management. Students examine the engineering principles of structural integrity for resilient environments.

prac.value: Application of digital modelling skills using SketchUp. Students begin translating 2D conceptual sketches into a 3D environment, focusing on accurate scaling and the placement of critical life-support systems. Instruction on the use of components and layers to organise complex structural data.

assessment.value: Formative monitoring of digital model progress and alignment with the initial design brief constraints.

outlineTheme.value: Iterative development of structural systems and interior resource zones.

keyTeachingExperiences.value:
Theory: Critical analysis of sustainability through three lenses: ecological (resource preservation), economic (long-term viability), and social (human wellbeing and ethics). Students explore 'life cycle thinking' in the context of material selection for extreme environments.

Prac: Refinement of the digital shelter model. Students apply textures and materials to represent physical properties (e.g., thermal mass, insulation). Development of annotated graphical representations within the software to justify design decisions based on sustainability principles.

resources.value: Sustainability framework handouts, material property database, SketchUp Styles and Materials browser.

theory.value: Critical analysis of sustainability through three lenses: ecological (resource preservation), economic (long-term viability), and social (human wellbeing and ethics). Students explore 'life cycle thinking' in the context of material selection for extreme environments.

prac.value: Refinement of the digital shelter model. Students apply textures and materials to represent physical properties (e.g., thermal mass, insulation). Development of annotated graphical representations within the software to justify design decisions based on sustainability principles.

assessment.value: Drafting of the sustainability justification report; teacher conferencing to review alignment with ecological and social criteria.

outlineTheme.value: Applying project management plans to coordinate the digital production process.

keyTeachingExperiences.value:
Theory: Introduction to project management and evaluation techniques. Students define criteria for success based on the design brief and explore how to measure the effectiveness of a designed solution against identified needs and opportunities.

Prac: Finalisation of the project management plan, including sequenced time and action plans. Students generate 2-dimensional orthogonal and 3-dimensional perspective views from their SketchUp models for inclusion in their technical report. Evaluation of the design against the established criteria.

resources.value: Project management templates, evaluation rubrics, SketchUp 'Scenes' tool instructions.

theory.value: Introduction to project management and evaluation techniques. Students define criteria for success based on the design brief and explore how to measure the effectiveness of a designed solution against identified needs and opportunities.

prac.value: Finalisation of the project management plan, including sequenced time and action plans. Students generate 2-dimensional orthogonal and 3-dimensional perspective views from their SketchUp models for inclusion in their technical report. Evaluation of the design against the established criteria.

assessment.value: Internal moderation of project management plans and preliminary evaluation drafts.

outlineTheme.value: Evaluating design effectiveness against ecological, economic, and social criteria.

keyTeachingExperiences.value:
Theory: Communication of technical solutions to a specific audience. Students examine how to present complex engineering and sustainability data clearly using digital tools, ensuring all graphical representations adhere to design conventions.

Prac: Compilation of the final digital portfolio. Students integrate their SketchUp models, annotated diagrams, project plans, and evaluations into a professional technical response. Peer review of digital solutions to identify strengths and areas for future design iteration.

Assessment: Submission of Assessment 3: Doomsday Shelter Project.

resources.value: Digital portfolio templates, LMS submission portal, final assessment checklist.

theory.value: Communication of technical solutions to a specific audience. Students examine how to present complex engineering and sustainability data clearly using digital tools, ensuring all graphical representations adhere to design conventions.

prac.value: Compilation of the final digital portfolio. Students integrate their SketchUp models, annotated diagrams, project plans, and evaluations into a professional technical response. Peer review of digital solutions to identify strengths and areas for future design iteration.

outlineTheme.value: Communicating the final designed solution and evaluating the design process.
